title: EpiGNN: Epistasis-Aware Multi-Mutation Protein Fitness Prediction via Message Passing on Language Model-Derived Residue Coupling Graphs
description: We propose EpiGNN, a lightweight graph neural network that predicts epistatic corrections to additive protein language model fitness scores. By constructing mutation-centric subgraphs where edges represent PLM-derived residue couplings, EpiGNN models how co-occurring mutations interact through structural and functional coupling networks, improving multi-mutation fitness prediction beyond additive baselines.
motivation: Current protein language models predict multi-mutation fitness effects by summing independent single-mutation scores, ignoring epistatic interactions. This additive assumption systematically fails for combinatorial mutations, which are the primary focus of protein engineering. Recent work shows PLMs encode pairwise epistasis in their internal representations but this information is not exploited by standard scoring approaches. A dedicated method to extract and leverage these coupling signals for non-additive fitness prediction would fill a critical gap in computational protein engineering.
proposed_approach: EpiGNN operates in four steps: (1) Extract per-residue embeddings and attention-based coupling scores from ESM-2 for each protein. (2) For each multi-mutant, construct a small mutation subgraph where nodes are mutated positions with mutation-induced embedding changes as features, and edges are weighted by PLM-derived residue couplings. (3) Apply 2-3 layers of graph attention message passing to propagate mutation effects through the coupling network. (4) Predict an epistatic correction term that is added to the additive PLM score. The model is trained on multi-mutation DMS data from ProteinGym to minimize fitness prediction error. The mutation-centric graph is key: it is small (only mutated positions), lightweight, and focused on inter-mutation interactions rather than whole-protein structure.

related_work.title: Evolutionary-scale prediction of atomic-level protein structure with a language model
related_work.authors: Lin, Z., Akin, H., Rao, R., et al.
related_work.relevance: ESM-2 serves as our backbone PLM for feature extraction and baseline fitness scoring. We build on top of ESM-2 embeddings and attention maps.

related_work.title: Protein Language Models Capture Structural and Functional Epistasis in a Zero-Shot Setting
related_work.authors: Nambiar, A., Littlefield, S.B., Cuellar, C., Khorana, R., & Maslov, S.
related_work.relevance: Shows PLMs encode pairwise epistasis in attention maps. Our work operationalizes this finding into a predictive model and extends to higher-order interactions via message passing.

related_work.title: A protein dynamics-based deep learning model enhances predictions of fitness and epistasis
related_work.relevance: Uses MD-derived dynamics coupling (DCIasym) with GNN for epistasis prediction. We replace expensive MD simulations with PLM-derived coupling, achieving similar structural priors at a fraction of the computational cost.

related_work.title: Protein stability models fail to capture epistatic interactions of double point mutations
related_work.relevance: Demonstrates that current models (including deep learning) fail at epistasis prediction, with additive models performing comparably. Motivates the need for architectures that explicitly model non-additive interactions.

related_work.title: Generalizable and scalable protein stability prediction with rewired protein generative models (SPURS)
related_work.relevance: State-of-the-art stability prediction using rewired ESM-2 + ProteinMPNN. Focuses on stability (ddG) rather than general fitness, and does not use mutation-centric graphs.

related_work.title: ProteinGym: Large-scale benchmarks for protein fitness prediction and design
related_work.authors: Notin, P., Kollasch, A.W., Ritter, D., et al.
related_work.relevance: Provides the primary benchmark datasets for evaluation, including multi-mutation DMS assays across diverse protein families.

related_work.title: On Recovering Higher-order Interactions from Protein Language Models
related_work.relevance: Theoretical analysis of higher-order interaction recovery from PLMs. Our work provides a practical architecture to exploit these interactions for fitness prediction.

related_work.title: Language models enable zero-shot prediction of the effects of mutations on protein function
related_work.authors: Meier, J., Rao, R., Verkuil, R., et al.
related_work.relevance: Introduced ESM-1v and the masked marginal scoring approach for zero-shot fitness prediction, which serves as our additive baseline.

related_work.title: MSA Transformer
related_work.authors: Rao, R., Liu, J., Verkuil, R., et al.
related_work.relevance: Demonstrated that PLM attention maps encode protein contact information, which we leverage for constructing residue coupling graphs.

related_work.title: Adaptation in protein fitness landscapes is facilitated by indirect paths
related_work.authors: Wu, N.C., Dai, L., Olson, C.A., Lloyd-Smith, J.O., & Sun, R.
related_work.relevance: Provided the GB1 combinatorial fitness landscape, one of the richest multi-mutation datasets demonstrating pervasive epistasis.

hypothesis: A graph neural network operating on mutation-centric subgraphs with PLM-derived residue coupling edges will significantly improve multi-mutation protein fitness prediction over additive PLM baselines (>5% improvement in Spearman correlation), with improvement magnitude correlating with the epistatic complexity of the target protein.
success_criteria: EpiGNN achieves statistically significant improvement (p < 0.05) in Spearman correlation over additive ESM-2 baseline on at least 70% of multi-mutation DMS assays in ProteinGym.
Ablation removing graph structure (MLP-only) performs significantly worse than full EpiGNN, confirming the value of the coupling graph.
PLM-derived coupling edges outperform random edges in the graph ablation, validating that attention-based coupling encodes meaningful interaction information.
The epistasis correlation metric shows the model captures non-additive effects beyond what additive baselines predict.
Cross-protein generalization: the model transfers to unseen proteins with positive improvement over additivity.

key_components: Attention extraction and coupling computation: <30 minutes
GNN training (5-fold CV per protein): 1-2 hours
